does: This paper introduces SoftSAE, a sparse autoencoder that replaces the fixed sparsity constraint of standard TopK SAEs with dynamic, input-dependent feature selection. Rather than activating exactly K features for every input, SoftSAE learns to select a varying number of active features based on input complexity. The method uses a soft gating mechanism that allows the model to adaptively choose relevant features while maintaining sparsity as a constraint through a learned temperature parameter. The paper demonstrates that this dynamic approach better captures the intrinsic dimensionality of different input regions, reducing unnecessary feature activation for simple samples while preserving capacity for complex cases.
connects: This work builds on the growing field of sparse autoencoders for mechanistic interpretability, which have recently gained prominence as tools for decomposing polysemantic neural activations into interpretable monosemantic features. SoftSAE directly extends TopK SAE architectures, which enforce fixed sparsity levels across all inputs. The paper addresses a fundamental limitation of prior SAE work by incorporating insights from manifold learning—the observation that natural data often exhibits varying local intrinsic dimensionality. This connects to broader research on adaptive neural representations and variable-capacity models that adjust complexity to match local data structure, principles established in statistical learning theory and information geometry.
enables: This approach opens new directions for interpretability research by enabling more faithful representations of how neural networks actually process inputs of varying difficulty. Dynamic sparsity could improve SAE utility for understanding phase transitions in language models where computation shifts across different feature sets. The framework also suggests pathways toward more efficient mechanistic interpretability tools that don't waste capacity on simple cases, potentially enabling deployment of interpretability methods at scale. Future work could extend dynamic selection to hierarchical feature decomposition, investigate how optimal sparsity varies during learning, or apply similar principles to other interpretability techniques like attention pattern analysis.
